safe_answer_rules: Cite only V4 nodes (sections, subsections, tables) that this workflow lists in primary_sections / supporting_tables.
If user input is missing for a required_input, ask the user instead of fabricating an assumption.

If a referenced section/table is in missing_or_parking_lot_refs, state that the source is not currently in the V4 brain and either point to PDF or surface adjacent available content with explicit caveat.
Never invent a numeric threshold; only quote thresholds from threshold_candidates with confidence not equal to 'low'.
When asked about an orphan parent, surface the children list and parent_link only — never invent a parent body.
Always include source_pdf + page_start in citation lines.
If sprinkler_protected is required for a calculation but not provided, ASK.
Use Table 1006.2.1 for 'spaces with one exit' decisions; Table 1006.3.3 for 'minimum number of exits per story' decisions.
Travel-distance values must come from the cited table row, not inferred from text.
must_not_claim_rules: Do not claim coverage of refs in missing_or_parking_lot_refs.
Do not assert engineering conclusions (e.g., 'sprinkler X requires alarm Y') unless an explicit cross-reference edge exists in relationships.json.
Do not paraphrase code text; quote verbatim from V4 chunk content.
Do not produce model-generated body for orphan / unadopted refs.
Do not generate decision trees that combine SBC 201 and SBC 801 requirements unless an explicit cross-reference (xref) edge ties them.
Do not claim that an exit is 'sufficient' without citing the specific table row that supports the claim.
citation_requirements: Every cited section/table must include: code, ref, title, page_start, source_pdf
Every threshold quoted must include: value + unit + section_ref + page_start
If parking-lot ref is referenced, citation must say 'not in V4 corpus — see PDF page X' (page derived from PDF table of contents only, not invented).

in day care. For example, an outpatient clinic that is open 24 hours a day is a Group B occupancy, provided care recipients are treated as o
and there are no inpatients who would stay at the facility 24 hours or longer. In the Group I-2 setting, care recipients are in for several
Another example would be a “day care” facility that is open 24 hours to serve workers who work any shift and need to have children in “day c
rovided that individual children receive care for less than 24 hours, the occupancy would be classified as Group I-4 or possibly Group E. Ea
ticular, one- and twofamily dwellings account for more than 80 percent of all deaths from fire in residential occupancies and about two-thir
es. One- and twofamily dwellings also account for more than 80 percent of residential property losses from fire and more than one-half of al
